card.title: Confirmation Bias
card.teaser: Why your brain hunts for evidence that you're already right — and quietly ignores the rest.
card.coverImagePrompt: A silhouetted head in profile with a magnifying glass inside, searching through scattered papers, all cheerfully ignoring a large contradictory document behind them, editorial illustration, warm amber palette

lessons.title: A three-number puzzle
lessons.body: In 1960, psychologist Peter Wason showed people the sequence 2, 4, 6 and said: 'Guess the rule.' Volunteers could propose more sequences to test. Almost everyone proposed confirming examples — 8, 10, 12 — and concluded the rule was 'even numbers going up by two.' The actual rule was much broader: 'any increasing sequence.' They never thought to test 5, 10, 11 — because humans are deeply reluctant to ask whether they might be wrong.

visual.imagePrompt: A row of three large numbered cards — 2, 4, 6 — on a wooden table with a thinker's silhouette in the background

lessons.title: What confirmation bias actually is
lessons.body: Confirmation bias is the tendency to seek, interpret, and remember information in a way that supports what we already believe. It has three flavours: selective search (we Google things we expect to confirm us), biased interpretation (we scrutinise opposing evidence more harshly), and selective recall (we remember the hits and forget the misses). It's not stupidity — it's the default setting of a brain built to act, not to deliberate.

keyTerms.definition: A tendency to favour information that supports existing beliefs
Information that could prove a belief wrong — the test most people avoid

visual.imagePrompt: Three filing cabinets labelled Search, Interpret, Remember, each overflowing with papers in a single colour and ignoring a colourful one

lessons.title: Why evolution likes the bias
lessons.body: Confirmation bias looks like a flaw from a logician's desk, but from an evolutionary standpoint it's economical. If your tribe believes the red berries are poisonous, the cost of confirming that belief is low. The cost of disconfirming it — taking a bite — can be lethal. Our brains evolved to be efficient at surviving tomorrow, not optimal at being right.

visual.imagePrompt: A hand hesitating above wild berries on a bush, split composition with one person cautiously backing away and another lying sick, editorial illustration

lessons.title: It infects experts too
lessons.body: A famous 1979 Stanford study showed subjects two studies on capital punishment — one supporting deterrence, one rejecting it. Both sides rated the study that agreed with them as methodologically superior and left with their views strengthened, even though they saw the same evidence. Physicians, judges, and intelligence analysts all perform worse on confirmation tasks when cases touch beliefs they hold strongly.
lessons.funFact: In World War II, statistician Abraham Wald saved countless lives by noticing that the military wanted to armour the places on returning planes where bullets had hit — when really they should have armoured the places without damage, because planes hit there never came back. Classic confirmation trap.

visual.imagePrompt: A vintage WWII bomber with red dots marking bullet holes around the wings and fuselage — but none on the engines or cockpit, diagrammatic

lessons.title: How to pre-commit to doubt
lessons.body: You can't talk yourself out of confirmation bias in the moment — your mind is already captured. The only reliable defences are structural: write down predictions before you see results, actively seek the strongest counterargument, and phrase questions as 'what would prove me wrong?' Charles Darwin kept a notebook specifically for observations that contradicted his theories. He called them his 'golden facts.'

quiz.question: What did Wason's 2-4-6 task demonstrate?
options: People are bad at mental arithmetic
People rarely test their own hypotheses with disconfirming examples
People prefer small numbers to large ones
People are good at inductive reasoning
quiz.explanation: Most volunteers only tried sequences that fit their guess. They almost never asked what would falsify their idea.

quiz.question: Which of the following is NOT a form of confirmation bias?
options: Interpreting ambiguous data in favour of your belief
Remembering confirming examples better than contradicting ones
quiz.explanation: Random sampling is exactly what confirmation bias is not — it's unbiased by definition.

quiz.question: What was Darwin's method for fighting his own bias?
options: He wrote down observations that contradicted his theories
He refused to publish controversial ideas
quiz.explanation: Darwin kept a dedicated notebook for 'golden facts' — the disconfirming observations he was most tempted to forget.

sources.title: On the failure to eliminate hypotheses in a conceptual task
Biased Assimilation and Attitude Polarization
sources.publisher: Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
